title: The $2M Award: Changing Guidelines for Detransitioners
content:
Thousands of American teenage girls have undergone elective mastectomies as part of gender-transition treatments in recent years. Advocates argue that these procedures can relieve severe distress and even prevent youth suicide. However, critics claim the medical evidence supporting these surgeries is shoddy and predict a wave of medical malpractice lawsuits. This legal threat was highlighted recently when a single patient in New York was awarded $2 million. The case centered around Fox Varian, a young woman who was prescribed an irreversible double mastectomy at the age of 16.

After a difficult childhood, Ms. Varian struggled with autism, depression, and anxiety, eventually identifying as a boy at age 15. Her psychologist allegedly warned her mother that the teenager was at high risk of suicide without the transition surgery. Despite undergoing the procedure, Varian's mental health worsened, and she chose to detransition in 2022. She subsequently filed a malpractice lawsuit, testifying that she was severely mentally ill when the surgery was performed. The jury ultimately awarded her compensation for past and future pain, marking the first successful lawsuit of its kind by a detransitioner.

Following this verdict, the American Society of Plastic Surgeons (ASPS) formally opposed gender-related surgeries for patients under 19. The ASPS cited the poor quality of existing studies, potential harms, and a lack of evidence that the benefits outweigh the risks. The American Medical Association quickly echoed this stance, agreeing that surgical interventions for minors should generally be deferred until adulthood. These warnings align with international findings, such as Britain's Cass Review, which also deemed the evidence for youth transition procedures remarkably weak. Consequently, observers believe this case could open the floodgates for hundreds of millions of dollars in similar lawsuits.
